assertions.text: Trigger detection, shipment optimisation, and companion constraint are in separate tables. Not one monolithic table.
assertions.evidence: Response has three separate tables: '1. Order Shipment Grouping', '2. Warehouse Selection (Minimising Shipments)', and '3. Companion Product Co-location', each addressing a distinct concern.

assertions.text: At least one row where multiple triggers fire simultaneously (e.g. mixed fulfilment AND mixed stock status).
assertions.evidence: 'All three split triggers at once | Shirt (home, in stock, 123 Main) + Mug (pickup, in stock, Store A) + Book (home, pre-order, 123 Main) | 3 | Each group separate' — fires mixed fulfilment type and mixed stock status simultaneously.

assertions.text: All four split triggers are covered: mixed fulfilment, mixed stock status, no single warehouse has all items, different addresses.
assertions.evidence: Mixed fulfilment: 'Pickup alongside home delivery'; Mixed stock status: 'In-stock item + pre-order'; Different addresses: 'Personal item + gift, different addresses'; No single warehouse: Table 2 rows 'Only a two-warehouse combination works' and 'No two-warehouse solution; three required'.

assertions.text: The 'no warehouse has both companions' case is included and recognised as a product decision — e.g. flagged as an open question, or the chosen resolution is explained. Not silently omitted.
assertions.evidence: 'Companions at different warehouses, no overlap | Camera body + 50mm lens | yes | Chicago: body only, LA: lens only | no | Co-location impossible; ships separately' — resolution is explicitly stated.

assertions.text: Companion constraint covers at least: companions at same location, companions split from non-companion items, and the ambiguous case (no location has both).
assertions.evidence: Same location: 'Both companions at same warehouse'; Non-companions split: 'Non-companions split across warehouses | Shirt + Coffee mug | no | Chicago: shirt, LA: mug | no'; No location has both: 'Companions at different warehouses, no overlap'.

assertions.text: Optimisation scenarios describe the business situation (e.g. 'Consolidate: two beats three', 'Everything at one warehouse') — not 'Test case 2'.
assertions.evidence: Scenario names include: 'One warehouse covers full quantity', 'Two-warehouse solution exists; avoid three', 'Only a two-warehouse combination works', 'No two-warehouse solution; three required', 'Total stock across all warehouses falls short'.

assertions.text: The trigger table represents multi-item orders where each item has its own fulfillment type, address, and availability. The notation used makes the item-to-property mapping unambiguous — not value sets like {delivery, pickup} which lose which item has which property.
assertions.evidence: 'Pickup alongside home delivery | Shirt (home delivery, in stock) + Mug (store pickup, in stock) | 2 | Shirt → home delivery; Mug → store pickup' — each item is paired with its own properties inline.

assertions.text: Optimisation table uses a scalar count column (e.g. 'Shirts ordered') not item lists, when product identity doesn't matter for the logic.
assertions.evidence: Table 2 has a 'Quantity ordered' column with scalar values like '6 shirts' consistently across all rows, not item-by-item lists.

assertions.text: Inventory assignments are physically valid: items are only assigned to locations that have them. Shipment counts match the assignments shown.
assertions.evidence: Row 1: Chicago 6 assigned for order of 6 (valid). Row 2: Philly 3 + DC 3 = 6 (valid). Row 3: Chicago 4 + LA 2 = 6 (valid). Row 5: Chicago 3 + LA 2 + Houston 2 = 7 ≥ 6 (valid). Row 6: total stock 5 < 6, no assignment shown, flagged open. All shipment counts match assignments.

assertions.text: @DisplayName is present on test methods, OR method names read as clear descriptive titles when converted from camelCase/snake_case.
assertions.evidence: The response contains no Java or Kotlin test code whatsoever — it is a pure spec-by-example table analysis. No @DisplayName annotations or test method names appear anywhere.

assertions.text: @Description is present on at least the class and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as the splitting rules, constraints, or application context. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
assertions.evidence: The response contains no Java or Kotlin test code. No @Description annotation is present anywhere in the output.

assertions.text: Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
assertions.evidence: No annotations of any kind appear in the response; it contains only markdown tables and prose, no Java/Kotlin code.

assertions.text: @Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.
assertions.evidence: No @Description annotation or any Java/Kotlin code is present in the response.
